fast.summary: This survey paper examines bias and unfairness issues that emerge when large language models are integrated into information retrieval systems like search engines and recommenders. It unifies these issues as distribution mismatch problems and categorizes them across three LLM integration stages: data collection, model development, and result evaluation.
fast.key_idea: Bias and unfairness in LLM-powered IR systems can be systematically understood as distribution mismatches between desired and actual outputs, enabling principled mitigation via distribution alignment techniques.
fast.why_it_matters: As LLMs become the backbone of search and recommendation systems, unchecked biases can distort information ecosystems at scale, affecting what content gets surfaced, who benefits, and how evaluation metrics mislead practitioners into thinking systems are fair when they are not.
possible_use_cases: Auditing search engine ranking algorithms for demographic or content bias introduced by LLM-based rerankers
Designing fairer LLM-based recommendation systems that account for popularity bias and exposure fairness
Developing more robust evaluation frameworks that detect when LLM-as-judge systems exhibit positional or verbosity bias

deep.method_breakdown: This paper presents a comprehensive survey that unifies bias and unfairness issues in LLM-integrated IR systems as distribution mismatch problems, providing a taxonomy for mitigation strategies through distribution alignment. It systematically examines biases across three critical pipeline stages: data collection (training/retrieval data biases), model development (algorithmic and representation biases introduced by LLMs), and result evaluation (metric and feedback loop biases). The framework covers both search engines and recommender systems, cataloging definitions, characteristics, and mitigation strategies for each bias type.
deep.key_innovation: The key novelty is the unifying theoretical lens — framing all bias/fairness problems as distribution mismatches between observed data distributions and ideal/target distributions. This is more general than prior IR fairness surveys (which predated LLM integration) and allows a single conceptual framework to cover heterogeneous bias types (selection bias, popularity bias, position bias, hallucination-induced bias, sycophancy, etc.) that were previously treated as isolated phenomena. It is also the first comprehensive survey specifically addressing LLM-era IR bias, bridging the NLP and IR fairness literatures.
deep.technical_insight: The most important design choice is decomposing the LLM-IR pipeline into three distinct integration stages (data, model, evaluation) rather than treating the system as a monolith. This works because bias sources at each stage have different causal mechanisms and therefore require different mitigation strategies — e.g., data debiasing techniques (resampling, augmentation) are appropriate upstream, while post-hoc calibration and fairness-aware ranking are appropriate downstream. This staged decomposition prevents conflating symptoms with root causes.
deep.limitations: As a survey, it does not propose or empirically validate new algorithms, so its contributions are organizational rather than technical. The distribution-mismatch framing, while unifying, may oversimplify biases that are fundamentally normative or context-dependent (e.g., what counts as a 'target' distribution is itself contested). Coverage is limited to published work up to the survey date, and rapidly evolving LLM capabilities (e.g., reasoning models, agentic retrieval) may introduce bias types not yet cataloged. Mitigation strategies are reviewed but not benchmarked comparatively.
interview_questions: How does the paper define the relationship between bias and unfairness, and why is unifying them as distribution mismatch problems useful?
What are the three pipeline stages the paper uses to categorize bias in LLM-integrated IR systems, and what types of bias emerge at each stage?
How do LLMs introduce new bias risks compared to traditional IR systems, particularly during the model development stage?
What mitigation strategies does the survey identify for position bias and popularity bias in LLM-based recommender systems?
What open problems does the paper highlight as most critical for future research in LLM-era IR fairness?
interview_answers: Bias refers to systematic deviations in data or model outputs from some ground truth or ideal, while unfairness refers to differential treatment of groups. The paper unifies both as cases where the actual distribution of system outputs diverges from a desired target distribution — this is useful because it allows a common set of distribution alignment techniques (reweighting, regularization, adversarial training) to serve as a general mitigation toolkit across otherwise heterogeneous problem types.

The three stages are: (1) Data collection — biases from non-representative training corpora, feedback loops in interaction logs, and skewed retrieval data (e.g., popularity bias, selection bias, exposure bias); (2) Model development — biases introduced by LLM pretraining (stereotypes, sycophancy, positional sensitivity) and fine-tuning choices; (3) Result evaluation — biases in metrics (e.g., relying on biased human judgments or LLM-as-judge evaluators) and in feedback signals used for RLHF-style alignment.

Traditional IR systems had well-studied biases (position, popularity, presentation bias) but LLMs add qualitatively new risks: hallucination can fabricate authoritative-sounding but false information; sycophancy causes models to align outputs with perceived user preferences rather than facts; verbosity bias in LLM-as-judge evaluation rewards longer outputs regardless of quality; and the opacity of LLM internals makes bias attribution much harder than in traditional ranking models.

For position bias, mitigation strategies include counterfactual data augmentation, inverse propensity scoring, and training with shuffled item orderings to reduce sensitivity to presentation order. For popularity bias, techniques include long-tail regularization, exposure-aware loss functions, and causal debiasing methods that model the confounding effect of item popularity on observed interactions. Some LLM-specific approaches use prompt engineering to suppress recency/popularity signals explicitly.

The paper highlights: (1) lack of standardized benchmarks for measuring LLM-era IR bias across diverse demographics and query types; (2) tension between personalization and fairness — highly personalized LLM responses may amplify filter bubbles; (3) evaluation circularity when using biased LLMs to evaluate LLM-generated content; (4) the need for causal rather than correlational bias detection methods; and (5) fairness in agentic/RAG systems where retrieval and generation biases compound across multi-step pipelines.
